rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiff'S Motion For Reconsideration Of September 4, 2018 Order Sustaining Demurrer To Plaintiff'S First Cause Of Action (Civil Code Section 895 Et Seq) And To Reverse It Restoring Plaintiff'S First Cause Of Action For Violations Of Building Standards
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, line 3. PLAINTIFF MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM'S Motion For Reconsideration Of September 4, 2018 Order Sustaining Demurrer To Plaintiff's First Cause Of Action (Civil Code Section 895 Et Seq) And To Reverse It Restoring Plaintiff's First Cause Of Action For Violations Of Building Standards. *** Tentative Ruling - Part 1 of 3 *** The hearing will be at 9:00 am, not 9:30 am. Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration of the September 4, 2018 order sustaining the demurrer to plaintiff's first cause of action for violation of the Right to Repair Act is denied. The McNernery parties' request for CCP 128.7 sanctions is also denied. Treating the motion as an invitation to the court, per Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal. 4th 1094, to reconsider on its own motion the September 4, 2018 regarding the first cause of action, the court exercises its discretion to take another thorough review of the difficult question whether this condominium project is or is not covered by Civil Code 895-945.5, the legislation that is often referred to as "Right to Repair Act." Even if plaintiff had not provided additional materials which arguably bear on this difficult question, the court would be inclined to take another hard look at this question because of its apparent significance to this litigation and the uncertainty this court has had in the past in deciding this question of first impression. After yet another full review of the question, the court remains of the view that the better interpretation of the sentence "This title applies to original construction intended to be sold as an individual dwelling unit" in Civil Code 896 is that "conversion" of non-residential buildings to residential units is not covered by the Right to Repair Act. The court once again acknowledges that there is arguable merit to the opposing view proffered by plaintiff and would welcome published appellate authority resolving the question. Fairly construed, the quoted sentence establishes that the Right to Repair Act only applies to "original construction intended to be sold as an individual dwelling unit" and thus is inapplicable to "original construction" that was not intended to be sold for residential purposes. No other language in the Right to Repair Act purports to states the scope of coverage of the Act. Therefore, the issue on this motion turns on the interpretation of the statutory phrase "original construction." That phrase is not defined in the Right to Repair Act and thus needs to be construed per well-settled principles of statutory interpretation, including looking to the text itself, the ordinary meaning of the disputed language, the context of that language, other statutory language, the purposes of the legislation, and any relevant legislative history. (See. e.g., De Vries v. Regents of the University of California (2016) 6 Cal. App. 5th 574 (good discussion of commonly employed tools to construe uncertain legislative language)). Although none of these principles definitively resolves the issue whether "original construction" covers only the initial construction work done for residential purposes (as contended by plaintiff) or also includes any initial construction that was done for non-residential purposes (as contended by the McNerney parties), a textual analysis strongly favors the latter position. *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 3 *** =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiff'S Motion For Leave To File Fourth Amended Complaint To Supplement And Clarify Allegations Set Forth In The Second Cause Of Action For Negligence And Add Twelfth Cause Of Action For Intentional Failure To Disclose
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, line 3. PLAINTIFF MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM'S Motion For Leave To File Fourth Amended Complaint To Supplement And Clarify Allegations Set Forth In The Second Cause Of Action For Negligence And Add Twelfth Cause Of Action For Intentional Failure To Disclose. The hearing will be at 9:00 am, not 9:30 am. Plaintiff's motion for leave to file a fourth amended complaint is denied. Plaintiff, a condominium owners' association, lacks standing to sue for the wrongs alleged in the allegations sought to be added to the second cause of action and in the proposed new twelfth cause of action. Nothing in Civil Code 945 or 5980 confers standing on plaintiff to sue for alleged non-disclosure of information about the buildings or the units therein. Section 945 provides rights to an association for "purposes of this title" and confers standing on an association to enforce the "provisions ¿ set forth in this title." In both phrases "title" refers to Title 7 of Part 2 of Division 2 of the Civil Code, which is the legislation originally enacted as SB 800 in 2002 and is often referred to as the "Right to Repair Act." Since none of the new matters sought to be alleged by plaintiff are contained in or pertain to the Right to Repair Act, section 945 is inapplicable to the proposed new matters. Section 5980 is also inapplicable to the proposed new matters because those new matters do not pertain to enforcement of governing documents or property damage, but rather pertain to what should have been disclosed to the DRE, unit buyers and plaintiff. The allegations sought to be added to the second cause of action and the new twelfth cause of action are also time-barred as a matter of law. The "nature of the right sued upon" - referred to as the "gravamen" - of a claim determines the statute of limitations that applies to the claim. (Thomson v. Canyon (2011) 198 Cal. App. 4th 594, 606-607). The gravamen of the allegations sought to be added to the second cause of action are negligent misrepresentation and the gravamen of the new twelfth cause of action is intentional fraud. Contrary to plaintiff's argument, the gravamen of the new matters is not latent construction defects since those matters focus not on quality of construction but rather on what should have been disclosed to the DRE, unit buyers and plaintiff. The applicable statutes of limitations are CCP 339(1) and 338(d) respectively, which are two and three years. Since the alleged wrongdoing occurred more than three years before this lawsuit was filed, the proposed new allegations and twelfth cause of action are time-barred. There is no reasonable possibility that plaintiff could amend its proposed new allegations and twelfth cause of action so that it would have standing to pursue those matters or to render those matters invulnerable to a statute of limitations defense. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the McNerney parties is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The hearing will be at 9:00 am, not 9:30 am. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Mint Collection -410-418 Jessie Street Condominium Motion For Reconsideration Of September 4, 2018 Order Sustaining Demurrer To Plaintiff'S First Cause Of Action (Civil Code Section 895 Et Seq) And To Reverse It Restoring Plaintiff'S First Cause Of Action For Violations Of Building Standards (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Plaintiff Mint Collection -410-418 Jessie Street Condominium Motion For Reconsideration Of September 4, 2018 Order Sustaining Demurrer To Plaintiff's First Cause Of Action *** Tentative Ruling - Part 3 of 3 *** No other language in the Act nor any of the Act's purposes favor the positon advocated by plaintiff. The primary purposes of the Act are to establish a set of standards for construction of residential units, supplant the common law (particularly Aas v. Superior Court (2000) 24 Cal. 4th 627) in various ways, and to impose certain pre-litigation procedures (such as the right to repair by a defendant). (See generally McMillin Albany LLC v. Superior Court (2018) 4 Cal. 5th 241). None of these purposes inform the proper meaning of the phrase "original construction." The fact that the Act includes standards for portions of buildings such as foundations, walls and roofing that are commonly retained when non-residential buildings are "converted" to residential units cuts both ways. It is reasonable to assume either way that the authors of the legislation might have intended to exclude this "prior" construction because it is unfair to subject older construction to modern standards or to include it in the newly-established criteria for new residential units to require upgrading of systems that are now to be used for residential purposes. Nor is there any published case law that sheds light on the proper meaning of the phrase "original construction." Review of the legislative history on the original 2002 enactment of SB800 and the several amendments to the Act that is available on www.leginfo.ca.gov failed to disclose anything pertinent to what was intended by the phrase "original construction." In sum, the textual analysis that "original construction" means both original non-residential and residential construction is the best indicator of the Legislature's intent. The McNernery parties' request for CCP 128.7 sanctions is denied because the request for reconsideration of this difficult statutory interpretation issue was not frivolous or otherwise violative of section 128.7. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the McNerney parties is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The hearing will be at 9:00 am, not 9:30 am. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Mint Collection -410-418 Jessie Street Condominium Motion For Reconsideration Of September 4, 2018 Order Sustaining Demurrer To Plaintiff'S First Cause Of Action (Civil Code Section 895 Et Seq) And To Reverse It Restoring Plaintiff'S First Cause Of Action For Violations Of Building Standards (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Plaintiff Mint Collection -410-418 Jessie Street Condominium Motion For Reconsideration Of September 4, 2018 Order Sustaining Demurrer To Plaintiff's First Cause Of Action *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 3 *** To limit the phrase "original construction" to only initial construction work done for residential purposes would, in effect, re-write the unqualified phrase "original construction" to "original residential construction." Moreover, the "re-written" sentence of "This title applies to original residential construction intended to be sold as an individual dwelling unit" is a redundancy and contrary to normal English usage because all "original residential construction" is "intended to be sold" for residential purposes. So viewed, the "re-written version runs afoul of the long-established principle that every word of a statute should be given meaning if at all possible. Accordingly, a textual analysis strongly favors the position taken by the McNerney parties. This textual interpretation is not overcome by any of the other standard statutory interpretive tools. Plaintiff relies heavily on the sentence following the sentence that contains the phrase "original construction." That sentence reads "As to condominium conversions, this title does not apply to or does not supersede any other statutory or common law." Plaintiff construes that sentence as providing that the Right to Repair Act applies to the conversion of a building from non-residential use to residential use. Setting aside the contentious issue of whether a "condominium conversion" excludes the conversion of a building from non-residential use to residential use, the phraseology "this title does not apply to" cannot fairly be read, as plaintiff contends, as "this title [the Right to Repair Act] applies to." Stated another way, the sentence excluding "condominium conversions" from the coverage of the Right to Repair Act does not provide that the Act applies to all condominiums except "condominium conversions." A sentence excluding certain real property from legislation is not itself a statement that the non-excluded real property is covered by the legislation especially where, as here, there is an explicit sentence stating what is covered by the Act, i.e. "original construction intended to be sold as an individual dwelling unit." Nor does the sole sentence in Civil Code 938, which appears under the heading "Date of sale for applicability of title" that "This title applies only to new residential units where the purchase agreement [is] ¿ on or after January 1, 2003" provide an answer the question on this motion. Given its heading, section 938 cannot be read that all "new residential units," regardless of whether they were built from scratch or "converted" from a non-residential building, during the relevant time period are covered by the Right to Repair Act. The undefined phrase "new residential units" in a section of the Act that relates to the time period when the Act applies does not show that the Legislature intended the phrase "original construction" in section 896" to mean "original residential construction/" *** End of Part 2 - Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 3 of 3 *** =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: MIRNA GONZALEZ VS. KIM'S PRO NAILS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT KIM'S PRO NAILS' Motion To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No opposition filed. Motion granted. Sanctions of $300 awarded to Defendant payable by June 10, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MATTHEW D. GRAMLY ESQ ET AL VS. LAVATION STUDIO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses From Plaintiff Matthew Gramly With Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT MIDLINE DBA LAVATION's Motion To Compel Further Responses From Plaintiff Matthew Gramly With Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted in part. As to Form Interrogatory 14.1, Plaintiff Gramly shall provide an amended response that specifically identifies the codes or ordinances he contends that Defendants violated. As to Interrogatory No. 5, Plaintiff shall provide an amended responses listing all of the SF City departments that he contacted regarding the noise issues. As to the various RFPDs, Plaintiff shall provide a privilege log of any documents withheld on the basis of privilege. As to RFPD No. 9, Plaintiff shall produce any responsive documents or indicate that he does not possess them. The Court finds that the remainder of Plaintiff's responses are sufficient. The Court awards sanctions of $250 because Plaintiff's failure to timely respond to the discovery necessitated Defendants to file this motion and incur costs. Sanctions are payable by June 10, 2019. Supplemental responses to be provided by June 10, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PETER ENGLANDER VS. SOUTHERN MARKETING AFFILIATES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, line 9. DEFENDANT SOUTHERN MARKETING AFFILIATES, INC'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. OFF CALENDAR. The case was dismissed on March 18, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ** TRANSFERRED TO ORANGE COUNTY ** NORTHERN CALIFORNIA COLLECTION SERVICE, INC. VS. IBOT INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO TRANSFER
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, line 12. DEFENDANT IBOT INC'S MOTION TO TRANSFER. GRANT. No opposition filed. The court transfers this action to Orange County and plaintiff is to remit all required transfer fees. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: STANLEY NEL VS. UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Defendants Petition For Order To Arbitrate And For Dismissal Or Stay Of Action Pending Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Friday, May 17, 2019, line 13. DEFENDANTS UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO, DONALD HELLER'S Petition For Order To Arbitrate And For Dismissal Or Stay Of Action Pending Arbitration. Defendants' petition to compel arbitration and to stay this action is granted. The arbitration agreement applies to "any and all legal disputes" and FEHA disputes are arbitrable. (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 93-96.) The agreement's exception applies to administrative DFEH complaints, not FEHA suits. (See Roman v. Sup. Ct. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1462, 1475-76.) The claims against Provost Heller are arbitrable because he was the agent through whom the university acted. (Garcia v. Pexco, LLC (2017) 11 Cal.App.5th 782, 788.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
